data.title: Expert quits US HIV role, rebukes Trump global health approach
data.description:
Key points:
  • US science officer criticizes aid cuts and use of health funding as leverage over developing nations
  • State Department says Reid left after admitting he could not provide nonpartisan scientific advice
  • Reid warns of reduced funding, oversight issues and shift toward military spending over health aid

By Simon Lewis

The chief science officer for the U.S. flagship HIV/AIDS program left his role this week and criticized the Trump administration's cuts to foreign assistance and what he said was its use of aid as leverage for U.S. commercial interests.

Republican President Donald Trump last year dismantled the U.S. Agency for International Development, which previously oversaw most foreign aid programs, but officials said life-saving work mainly in developing African nations under the President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief, a bipartisan initiative created under George W. Bush's presidency, would continue.

Mike Reid, a practicing infectious disease physician who served as chief science officer for PEPFAR in the State Department's Bureau of Global Health Security and Diplomacy, said in a post on Substack on Monday he had stayed in the job for the past 18 months in hopes of preserving at-risk programs.

But he said funding for health programs overseas was being used as leverage over developing countries, citing a New York Times report last month that said the State Department was considering withholding assistance to help people with HIV in Zambia to push the country to sign a favorable critical-minerals deal with the U.S.

"When access to treatment or prevention becomes entangled with access to critical minerals or geopolitical positioning, the work is no longer what it claims to be," he wrote.

The work of global health was "inherently anti-fascist" and incompatible with the administration's "authoritarian" domestic trajectory, he added.

'AMERICA FIRST' HEALTH STRATEGY

The State Department, after seeing the post on Monday, told Reid his employment was being ended immediately, he told Reuters in a phone interview on Tuesday.

Asked about Reid's post, the State Department did not respond to his specific criticisms. A department spokesperson said Reid departed by mutual agreement after he "admitted he could no longer provide nonpartisan scientific advice."

"As in every administration, the president and his team set policy, and it is the duty of every person affiliated with the department to faithfully execute that policy," the spokesperson said.

The State Department last week published data showing that the number of people being tested for HIV dropped sharply last year amid interruptions to PEPFAR, which has been credited with saving 26 million lives and preventing HIV infections in 7.8 million babies born to HIV-infected mothers ​since its start in 2003.

The State Department spokesperson said Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio were "building to end the HIV/AIDS epidemic as they champion the transformational work happening under the America First Global Health Strategy," and noted that Reid praised some parts of the Trump administration's policy in his Substack post.

That referred to Reid's acknowledgment that the Trump administration's strategy seeks to have poorer nations take ownership of the fight against HIV/AIDS and other diseases in their countries through bilateral agreements. Reid also wrote, however, that the U.S. was doing so while reducing overall funding to those countries as U.S. military spending has increased and as Trump has launched a war with Iran.

Reid told Reuters that moving funding to government health bodies instead of nongovernmental organizations was the right approach, but that it was coming after many of the U.S. officials who previously monitored for corruption and other misuse of funds had lost their jobs.

"I am concerned at the speed and the lack of oversight," he said.

data.title: Australia shares likely to fall, NZ down
data.description:
Australian shares were likely to fall on Wednesday with probable losses in mining stocks on lower copper prices, while investors awaited quarterly production results from miner BHP due later in the day.

The local share price index futures (YAPcm1) fell 0.7%, a 34.4-point discount to the underlying S&P/ASX 200 index close. The benchmark closed unchanged on Tuesday.

New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index fell 0.2% in early trade.

data.title: Target Hospitality Shares Fall on $7M Secondary Offering
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of Target Hospitality fell after the company said one of its shareholders would offer 7 million shares in an underwritten secondary offering.

Shares fell 9.9% to $14.32 in after-hours trading Tuesday, after closing up 1.9% at $15.90. At close, the stock had nearly doubled in value year to date.

Target Hospitality, which provides vertically integrated modular accommodations and hospitality service, said the shares were being offered by entities controlled by TDR Capital.

The company said it wasn't offering any shares and won't receive proceeds.

The underwriters for the offering have a 30-day option to buy an additional 1.05 million shares.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: GE Vernova Stock Has Soared to the Moon. How Earnings Could Keep It in Orbit. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

GE Vernova stock has been on an incredible tear, as power-hungry artificial intelligence data centers spread across America. That raises the bar for its coming earnings report.

The power generation equipment provider is set to report first-quarter numbers on Wednesday morning. Wall Street analysts tracked by FactSet are looking for earnings per share of $1.97 from sales of $9.2 billion. Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, or Ebitda, is expected to be $770 million.

A year ago, GE Vernova reported earnings per share of $1.07 from sales of $8 billion, according to FactSet. Orders were $10.2 billion. Ebitda was $457 million.

Along with earnings, financial guidance should be updated, too. In January, GE Vernova management said they expect a 2026 Ebitda profit margin of about 12%, up from 8.4% in 2025. That implies Ebitda of about $5.3 billion. Wall Street, meanwhile, projects $5.8 billion.

For 2028, Ebitda margins should be about 20%, implying Ebitda of about $11.2 billion. Wall Street currently projects 2028 Ebitda closer to $11.9 billion. Things have simply gotten better-than-expected — and faster-than-expected: A year ago, the 2028 Ebitda estimate was closer to $7 billion.

Higher demand for power generation equipment and better pricing are responsible for much of the improvement. A lot of that is reflected in GE Vernova's share price.

Coming into the week, the stock has gained roughly 50% since the fourth-quarter earnings report and has climbed 15% since the fighting started in Iran.

The average analyst price target has gone to about $900 a share, up from $406 a year ago, while shares have race to roughly $1,000 from $290. The gains have left investors wondering how good can things get. More demand and higher pricing and expanded capacity could be coming on Wednesday.

How the stock will react is hard to say; shares rose 2.7% after the company reported fourth-quarter numbers in January. Options markets imply shares will move about 5% up or down after tomorrow's results.

Regardless of what happens, the Street is positive on the stock, with almost 80% of analysts who cover the company rating shares Buy.

"We believe GE Vernova's continued pricing momentum within gas power should continue to lead to strong order intake and margin expansion for years to come, increasing visibility into above-average growth and margins into the early-2030s," wrote JPMorgan analyst Mark Strouse in a preview report. He rates shares Buy and has an $1,150 price target for the stock.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.description:
